items.title: This version of story confuses several things. The Plaza Accord did not "break" Japan's development model. That model was already broken once very hig...
items.description_html:
This version of story confuses several things. The Plaza Accord did not "break" Japan's development model. That model was already broken once very high investment rates no longer corresponded to the need for a massive rebuilding of infrastructure, housing stock and manufacturing capacity after the devastation of war.

Japan's growth model, which was the right growth model after the war when it was severely underinvested in nearly every sector, had made itself (through its very success) obsolete by the 1970s, as Albert Hirschman explained would happen, although, he warned, policymakers would find it extremely difficult to change the model, and so would likely go on to create a very different and opposite set of imbalances.

That is exactly what happened in Japan (and, indeed, in every country that followed the high-saving/high-investment development model), with the resulting overinvestment showing up clearly in the continued very-high investment share of GDP through the early 1990s (among the highest in history until China surpassed it in the mid 1990s) and, even more clearly, in the surge in the country's debt burden.

The latter is key, because when nearly all debt creation is used to fund investment, as was the case in Japan, the country's debt burden cannot rise if the investment is on average productive for the economy. The fact that it rose so rapidly for so long is strong evidence of massive investment misallocation.

That is why for years Tokyo had devalued its currency in real terms, which is just another way of saying that it forced up the value of the currencies of its trade partners. It did this to minimize the consequences of domestic overinvestment in manufacturing capacity (by exporting highly subsidized excess capacity) and to help mitigate the debt impact of its domestic overinvestment.

That is also why to say the problem was the "sudden" revaluation of the yen misses the point. For nearly two decades Washington (and policymakers in many other capitals) had asked Tokyo for a gradual revaluation of the yen (i.e. it asked Japan to stop forcing the dollar to be overvalued), but as Tokyo was unable to embrace any meaningful appreciation of the yen as long as it refused to take the necessary steps to rebalance its domestic economy, this left the US with only two options.

First, it could do nothing and suffer from the continued overvaluation of the dollar imposed on it by the Bank of Japan's currency policies. And second, because the US could not intervene directly in its currency the way Japan could, it could use the threat of tariffs (the standard alternative to currency devaluation) to force a near-term revaluation of the yen.

It chose the latter, but revaluation rebalances the domestic economy by transferring income from manufacturers (net exporters) to households (net importers) thereby raising the household share of GDP.

Tokyo, however, subsequently reversed this transfer by lowering interest rates and expanding investment lending, which transferred income back from households (net lenders to the banking system) to manufacturers (net borrowers).

That is almost certainly why the Plaza Accord were ultimately so much more painful for Japan than for Germany – not because the Plaza Accord created the problem, but rather because Germany never suffered from nearly the same extent of overinvestment as Japan, and began to adjust after the revaluation, whereas Tokyo tried to put off adjustment for several years longer by igniting an asset bubble and exacerbating the investment bubble.

Beijing is clearly determined to avoid the fate of Japan, but I worry that by misinterpreting what happened in Japan in the 1980s, it risks doing even worse than Japan did.


The China Academy: 1/7
Pettis and Setser are right that bad investment + debt matters. But this is a strawman. The Chinese Plaza lesson is not “one treaty cursed Japan.” It is: don’t let external pressure force a sudden currency move that breaks your developmental model.

items.title: A very good Adam Tooze explanation of the current state of debate on Chinese imbalances. He is right, by the way, when he dismisses sectoral explanati...
items.description_html:
A very good Adam Tooze explanation of the current state of debate on Chinese imbalances. He is right, by the way, when he dismisses sectoral explanations (I call them “incremental” explanations) for the surge in overall imbalances by noting that “all macroeconomic dynamics, insofar as they involve any element of structural, “qualitative” change, are by definition, sectoral, industrial, or even corporate stories.”

To put it another way, you cannot explain a rise in China’s domestic macroeconomic imbalances (e.g. a rise in net exports) by pointing to a rise in a particular export (e.g. solar panels).

In a balanced economy, after all, more successful sales by solar-panel producers should result in rising employment, wages or investment that would, in turn, result in more imports by Chinese households and businesses.

The point is that in a well-functioning, balanced economy, in which wage growth is able to keep pace with productivity growth, a boost in exports of a particular sector or industry, or to a particular foreign client base, would not cause an equivalent boost in net exports but rather an equivalent boost in imports. What may look on the surface like a sectoral story (foreigners are buying more Chinese-made solar panels) is in fact a domestic imbalance story (rising production isn’t translating into rising wages and, thus, rising consumption).

This, as Tooze notes, has implication for EU policy. If China cannot or will not change domestic imbalances, the EU has two options. It can either do nothing, “But doing nothing is potentially destabilizing as well, Tooze writes. “Spectacular and uncontrolled deindustrialization must count as a serious economic, social and political risk in Europe.”

Or it can intervene in its external accounts to reverse the impact of Chinese intervention, although, as Tooze notes, “if trade protection and international industrial bargaining are indeed the best solution, then that risks compounding the disorder in world trade hitherto personified by Donald Trump.”

My only disagreement, of course, if that I would argue that the “disorder in world trade” occurred many decades before Donald Trump took the presidency. This is essentially what Joan Robinson warned must happen in her 1937 essay. Deficit economies will only accept beggar-thy-neighbor trade policies, she said, until the cost becomes too high, after which they will retaliate by themselves intervening in their external accounts.
@adam_tooze https://open.substack.com/pub/adamtooze/p/chartbook-454-china-shock-20-and?r=12ygf&utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web

items.title: Very interesting Caixin article on private-equity financing in China. According to a fund manger cited in the article, "state-backed investors have be...
items.description_html:
Very interesting Caixin article on private-equity financing in China. According to a fund manger cited in the article, "state-backed investors have become the main source of capital for China’s private equity industry in recent years."

This isn't about managing excess funds, however. There seems to be another, very different, reason why state-backed investors are so eager to get involved in private equity financing.

"Fund managers receiving government capital," the article notes, "agree to invest a specified amount back into the sponsoring region through factories, research centers, regional headquarters and other projects. The model has become increasingly important for local governments seeking to attract advanced manufacturing, create jobs and generate tax revenue."

In the past year, in response to rising criticism that part of China's global competitive advantage is access to near-unlimited amounts of cheap bank funding, some analysts have argued the data show this not to be the case. China's most competitive, high-tech industries, they say, are less likely than the average Chinese business to rely on bank borrowing, and more likely on private-equity financing. This implies, they say, that cheap financing is not part of the reason they are globally competitive.

But, as this article suggests, this may simply be a misreading of the way Chinese manufacturing is financed. It may be true that China's most competitive, high-tech industries don't have access to near-unlimited amounts of cheap funding through the banking system, but that may be mainly because they have it through private equity provided by local governments eager to see rapid manufacturing expansion in their jurisdictions.

This shouldn't surprise anyone. With among the lowest household shares of GDP in the world, Chinese manufacturing and Chinese growth should normally be extremely labor-intensive to take advantage of low unit labor costs. The fact that they are instead so capital intensive suggests that capital is even cheaper than labor.
https://www.caixinglobal.com/2026-06-30/in-depth-dreame-controversy-shines-spotlight-on-chinas-state-backed-funding-model-102459395.html?cxg=web&Sfrom=twitter

items.title: RT Michael Pettis: Setser is right, and I would add that those who think Japan's "lost decades" were caused by the Plaza Accord are engaged in some pr...
items.description_html:
RT Michael Pettis
Setser is right, and I would add that those who think Japan's "lost decades" were caused by the Plaza Accord are engaged in some pretty crude logical fallacies. After all the Plaza Accord didn't lead German to suffer the same "lost decades" as Japan, mainly because unlike Japan, Germany hadn't previously engaged in many years of unproductive investment (like Japan's famous "bridges to nowhere") and this unproductive investment hadn't been backed by a massive increase in the country's debt burden.

If you look at other countries that had investment-heavy growth models similar to that of Japan, most obviously the USSR and Brazil in the 1960s and early 1970s, they all suffered from crises or "lost decades", even though none of them entered into major, Plaza-Accord-style currency appreciations.

I know few analysts bother looking at the history of such growth models, but they should. If they did, they'd see that history doesn't tell us that any country that does a Plaza-Accord-style appreciation suffers from lost decades of growth (Germany and France didn't).

It tells us instead that any country that engages in many years of non-productive investment to keep GDP growth higher than the real economy can productively deliver, and that runs up huge corresponding debt burdens, is likely to suffer from lost decades as the excess capacity is gradually written down and the hidden losses allocated.

items.description_html:
RT Mid Day
Maharashtra’s proposed housing society rules aim to end disputes over maintenance charges by clearly defining which expenses must be shared equally and which should be linked to flat size across over 1.5 lakh societies.

Service charges, staff salaries and lift maintenance will be shared equally, while insurance, repair and sinking funds will be calculated based on flat size. The rules are expected to be notified in the coming months.

✍🏻: @vinodkumarmenon

items.title: The Snag At 10:45 in the morning, I was already in Bangalore. Not really. The plane hadn't moved. But in my head I was three meetings deep, downloadin...
items.description_html:
The Snag

At 10:45 in the morning, I was already in Bangalore.

Not really. The plane hadn't moved. But in my head I was three meetings deep, downloading one concall after another, treating the flight as nothing more than a useful window to get reading done. The journey was just a corridor between where I sat and where I had decided to be.

Then the pilot came on. Some technical snag.

I did what most of us do with inconvenient news. I ignored it. Snags get fixed. Schedules hold. IndiGo, of all airlines, the one obsessed with being on time, would surely not let ninety minutes vanish on a taxiway.

But the announcements kept coming. The plane, already rolling toward the runway, stopped. Then it turned back. Then it just sat there while engineers climbed aboard the same machine that was supposed to carry me through the sky. And somewhere at the back of my mind, years of watching aircraft investigation documentaries started whispering. That quiet little catalogue of small ignored faults that later became headlines.

Ninety minutes passed. And then something I still can't fully explain moved in me. Not fear exactly. More like conviction. My friend and I stood up, and I asked the air hostess, politely, almost apologetically, to let us off. I had no reason I could name. My gut had simply stopped negotiating. We had no check-in baggage, so they allowed it. We walked back to the bus and sat down.

Twenty minutes later, from outside the plane now, we watched the entire flight get cancelled.

The next one was in the evening. I went home.

What stayed with me was this.

I had built my whole morning on the quiet assumption that I was in charge of how it would go. The meetings, the reading, the arrival time, all of it rested on one silent belief. The plane will fly because I need it to. But the plane did not care what I needed. One fault I couldn't even see dissolved the entire shape of my day in a moment.

We confuse our plans with control. They are not the same thing. Planning is arranging the few things we can actually touch. Control is a story we tell ourselves about everything else. The transcripts and the calendar were mine. The engine was not. The weather was not. The thousand small things sitting between intention and arrival never were.

And here is the strange part. The moment I finally let go of the plan, stood up, walked off, and accepted home instead of Bangalore, the whole thing resolved on its own, almost as if it had only been waiting for me to loosen my grip.

That gut feeling deserves some credit too. We are not as in control as we like to think, but we are also not powerless. Somewhere between total mastery and total helplessness sits the one real thing we own, which is how we respond. I could not fix the plane. I could choose to listen to that quiet voice, get up, and walk away.

In the larger scheme of things, I am very small. A passenger, not a pilot, of most of my life. The honest move is not to pretend otherwise. It is to plan lightly, hold loosely, and trust the instinct that tells you when to walk back to the bus.

The meetings waited. The morning did not go as planned. And I reached home oddly grateful, reminded of the one thing no calendar can ever teach me. I was never the one flying the plane.

We are just tiny cogs in grander scheme of things. Always be grateful to beauty of life 🙂

Looking at the plane from the bus and realising how small we are in grander scheme of things

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: What is Stanley Druckenmillers Biggest position? It isn't a Tech, Space x or an AI co. It belongs to the Biotech sector. Nam...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
What is Stanley Druckenmillers Biggest position?

It isn't a Tech, Space x or an AI co. It belongs to the Biotech sector.

Name of the company is :- Natera

What the company does:

Natera is a genetic and molecular diagnostics company. In plain terms, it runs blood and DNA tests that help doctors make decisions in three areas: cancer (oncology), pregnancy (women's health), and organ transplants (organ health).

Its older flagship products include Panorama, a non-invasive prenatal test screening for chromosomal abnormalities in a fetus, and Horizon, a carrier screening test for hereditary conditions. But the real growth engine now is oncology. The business narrative shifted in early 2026 when the company hit a major milestone by generating $107.6 million in positive cash inflow in 2025, removing the dilution risk that had made hedge funds wary of its high cash burn.

Signatera — the blockbuster product:

Signatera is a personalized blood test that detects traces of cancer DNA left in the body after treatment. It belongs to a category called MRD (molecular/minimal residual disease) testing. The idea is simple but powerful: after a tumor is surgically removed, tiny amounts of cancer DNA can linger in the bloodstream long before a tumor is visible on a scan.

Signatera tests for any evidence of cancer cell DNA in a patient's blood and is used to stratify patients and guide therapy decisions after surgery, monitor how patients respond to treatment, and detect early cancer recurrence. It is the leading test in the MRD category and has been proven to meaningfully affect patient outcomes in colorectal, breast, and bladder cancer.

Adoption is the key bull signal: in early 2026, over 50% of all US oncologists had ordered a Signatera test in the previous quarter, and oncology test volumes grew 55% year-over-year in Q4 2025. Natera and bulls believe MRD testing can be a $20 billion market as oncologists keep adopting these tests and clinical utility is proven in more settings. Because cancer patients need repeated monitoring over time, each adopted patient becomes a recurring revenue stream and the feature that makes the long-term economics so attractive.

Razor Blade business model in Biotech, always fascinating to study unique companies.

Disclaimer: no recommendation to buy or sell.

items.title: The Sudarshan Chakra is India's planned multi-layered air and missile defence shield, announced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his 2025 Independen...
items.description_html:
The Sudarshan Chakra is India's planned multi-layered air and missile defence shield, announced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his 2025 Independence Day address.

Named after Lord Krishna's divine discus, it aims to build a nationwide protective umbrella against aerial threats including drones, cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, and aircraft.

The government has set a target of making the system operational by 2035, drawing inspiration partly from Israel's Iron Dome but on a far larger scale suited to India's size and diverse threats along its borders with Pakistan and China.

The concept envisions an integrated network of sensors, command centres, and interceptors working together to detect, track, and neutralise incoming threats at multiple ranges and altitudes. It is meant to protect critical infrastructure, military installations, cities, and economic assets.

Defence equipment likely to be needed includes:

Long range surface to air missile systems such as the S-400 Triumf and the indigenous Project Kusha, designed to engage targets at extended ranges.

Medium and short range air defence systems like the Akash, Akash NG, and the Barak 8 (MRSAM), for layered coverage closer to protected assets.

Ballistic missile defence interceptors, including the two tier Prithvi Air Defence and Advanced Air Defence systems for exo and endo atmospheric interception.

Counter drone and counter UAS systems, including directed energy weapons (laser based systems) and electronic warfare jammers to tackle swarm drone threats.

Advanced radar and sensor networks, such as Swordfish long range tracking radars, AEW&C aircraft, and space based surveillance satellites for early detection.

Integrated command and control infrastructure to fuse data from all layers and coordinate interception in real time.

Very short range systems and point defence guns for terminal protection of high value targets.

Defence businesses in Radars, electronics, anti drone systems & those involved in indigenisation of SAM systems will see big traction over the next few years.

items.title: What is Stanley Druckenmillers Biggest position? It isn't a Tech, Space x or an AI co. It belongs to the Biotech sector. Name of the company is :- Nat...
items.description_html:
What is Stanley Druckenmillers Biggest position?

It isn't a Tech, Space x or an AI co. It belongs to the Biotech sector.

Name of the company is :- Natera

What the company does:

Natera is a genetic and molecular diagnostics company. In plain terms, it runs blood and DNA tests that help doctors make decisions in three areas: cancer (oncology), pregnancy (women's health), and organ transplants (organ health).

Its older flagship products include Panorama, a non-invasive prenatal test screening for chromosomal abnormalities in a fetus, and Horizon, a carrier screening test for hereditary conditions. But the real growth engine now is oncology. The business narrative shifted in early 2026 when the company hit a major milestone by generating $107.6 million in positive cash inflow in 2025, removing the dilution risk that had made hedge funds wary of its high cash burn.

Signatera — the blockbuster product:

Signatera is a personalized blood test that detects traces of cancer DNA left in the body after treatment. It belongs to a category called MRD (molecular/minimal residual disease) testing. The idea is simple but powerful: after a tumor is surgically removed, tiny amounts of cancer DNA can linger in the bloodstream long before a tumor is visible on a scan.

Signatera tests for any evidence of cancer cell DNA in a patient's blood and is used to stratify patients and guide therapy decisions after surgery, monitor how patients respond to treatment, and detect early cancer recurrence. It is the leading test in the MRD category and has been proven to meaningfully affect patient outcomes in colorectal, breast, and bladder cancer.

Adoption is the key bull signal: in early 2026, over 50% of all US oncologists had ordered a Signatera test in the previous quarter, and oncology test volumes grew 55% year-over-year in Q4 2025. Natera and bulls believe MRD testing can be a $20 billion market as oncologists keep adopting these tests and clinical utility is proven in more settings. Because cancer patients need repeated monitoring over time, each adopted patient becomes a recurring revenue stream and the feature that makes the long-term economics so attractive.

Razor Blade business model in Biotech, always fascinating to study unique companies.

Disclaimer: no recommendation to buy or sell.

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Why NEW is a powerful word in investing New Earnings Power. A sharp acceleration in current quarterly EPS/sales vs. the same...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Why NEW is a powerful word in investing

New Earnings Power. A sharp acceleration in current quarterly EPS/sales vs. the same quarter last year. Not just growth but a new level of earnings the company hasn't printed before.

ANew Earnings Run-Rate. Annual earnings inflecting onto a higher trajectory (and here's where your New Margin Run-Rate fits perfectly — margin expansion driving a structurally higher profit pool, not a one-off).

New Products / New Management / New Highs. The original "N," untouched. The catalyst: a new product, new leadership, new capacity, new addressable market culminating in the stock making new price highs.

New Supply-Demand Setup. A new scarcity in the float: buybacks, low/shrinking share count, tightening supply against rising demand.

New Market Leaders. Buy the relative-strength leaders, not the laggards — the names showing new leadership within their group.

New Institutional Owners. Fresh, high-quality institutional sponsorship coming in & new funds building positions, not stale holders.

New Market Trend. The general market itself turning into a new uptrend (since 3 of 4 stocks follow the market).

items.title: Agentic routing is such an interesting study. Built a basic agent that does planning on Opus 4 but reasons on 4.6 through a tool call (skill call). Du...
items.description_html: Agentic routing is such an interesting study. Built a basic agent that does planning on Opus 4 but reasons on 4.6 through a tool call (skill call). Due to lack of document grounding, I embedded corpus into system prompt. Worked well. Second stage, enabled managed RAG for grounding & agent stated skipping tool call entirely, no skill calls. It treated grounded data as a higher evidence deterministic choice & skipped skill call. Tweaked tool description, still no skill call. Changed skill description matching to tool call & bingo!! Agent skipped grounding, entered planning mode & made a tool call. Evals for Agentic behavior is literally out paces everything in this field. What's your such key learning from a recent build?

items.title: A lot of defense to IT SIs right now is based on assumption that enterprise clients will not open their systems, data & processes to external AI solut...
items.description_html: A lot of defense to IT SIs right now is based on assumption that enterprise clients will not open their systems, data & processes to external AI solutions (Claude, OpenAi). However, this defense collapses the moment we have a precedence from a big enterprise opening up just the way enterprises opened up to cloud after a decade long friction. The moment this starts happening, the JVs OpenAi & Claude have formed will make a killing. And, what will take this wall down? For e.g., enterprises are currently very upset after SAP API policy that prohibited clients from using their own data & sending it to external tools via APIs to architect a solution outside of SAP. This can be the 1st hammer in the wall. One wrong step & clients will find ways to disconnect with enterprise OEMs & will be willing to trust(with an agreement perhaps) likes of Claude & OpenAi. The moment this happens, it is done!! This is the biggest risk I foresee.

This framework wasn't built in a weekend.

It is the product of decades of investing, countless mistakes, thousands of hours of reading, writing, thinking and refining my own mental models.

AI didn't invent the framework.

It helped me organise and express years of accumulated judgement into something practical, testable and continuously improvable.

I think of AI as infrastructure.

Like a road.

The road doesn't decide where you go.

The destination depends on who's driving, what vehicle they're driving, and the judgement they bring to every turn.

This framework is Version 5.0 today.

Hopefully, it will never be finished.

items.description_html:
RT M B Patil
I welcome the first PCB Electronics Manufacturing unit in North Karnataka.

@LionCircuits, Thank you for being a Part of the Unbelievable Transformation story of Vijayapura. From a drought region 10 years ago, It has seen a Water Revolution, Green Revolution, Education Revolution and now an Employment Revolution.

Vijayapura stands testament to be one of India's few districts to have undergone such a massive 360 degree transformation in such a short period.

The 100-acre Integrated PCB Mega Campus marks the beginning of a new chapter in Karnataka's electronics manufacturing journey.

Be assured of my personal attention and the full support of my department. We will extend every possible assistance to ensure this game-changing project is implemented smoothly and successfully.

Today, the district is ready for an industrial revolution, with investments in electronics, renewable energy, beverages, advanced manufacturing, and several other sectors gathering momentum.

May this project become a benchmark for India's electronics manufacturing ambitions. We look forward to building a future defined by innovation, employment, and shared prosperity.

Best wishes to the Terra Circuits team.
@MuraliSrinivasa

items.description_html:
This is the site where our new 100 acre Integrated PCB mega campus will be built in 🇮🇳

We have been working hard to fix the structual gaps in PCB manufacturing in 🇮🇳

Some of the problems are harder than we think and it takes time to fix those.
We have been working on fixing all those problems that every single customer has told us over time and we work hard to make sure that each of those are fixed by identifying the root cause.

Our dedicated team of Terra Circuits will be focused on some of these infrastructre related problems.

PCB Fabs- Inspired by the way TI expanded semicon fabs in Dallas, we will be taking a similar approch here. There will be multiple Fab Units that will be built over time in this campus.

PCBA- we will have enough lines to support our customers scaling to larger numbers.

Components warehouse- a fully automated warehouse to ship components the same or next day.

R&D center- to research and build technologies related to manufacturing, not just pcb. Semicon and advanced applied engg.

I am dedicating my next 10 years building this campus and change the way electronics gets manufactured in 🇮🇳

#JaiHind

items.title: RT Sagar Awatade: I don't know who else to tell this to, so I am going to tell my story here. Every day is a struggle for a young business, but the la...
items.description_html:
RT Sagar Awatade
I don't know who else to tell this to, so I am going to tell my story here.

Every day is a struggle for a young business, but the last few months have been harder than usual.

We are a small Indian company. For more than ten years we have been building a homegrown brand in a product category dominated by big foreign players.

There are almost no Indian names in this space. We set out to be one.

We started in 2014. Over the years we began making parts in India instead of just importing, and we started selling in the US, Dubai, Nepal, Malaysia and South Africa.

We showed up at global trade fairs to represent an Indian brand on the world stage.

In 2023 we changed the import code we use for our product. We did not do this quietly. Every shipment was declared. Nothing was hidden. We didn't invent our approach.

We followed written professional advice and the way this product is treated in markets around the world.

And now we are facing a government demand running into tens of crores in duty recovery and penalties, plus personal penalties on the founders and even on an employee.

For a company our size, this is not a fine we can pay and move on from. This ends us.

We have not run from any of this. I am not built like that. It is not how I was raised. We have written to the authorities, met officials in person, and we have now filed a writ in the High Court.

All we are asking for is a fair treatment.

I set out to build in India and sell to the world. I am asking only that the system back honest founders trying to compete globally, instead of breaking them.

The process is the process, and it exists for a reason. But process should not feel like punishment.

From where I am standing today, it does.
I am not giving up. I have worked too hard for this. If you have read this far, please share it. If you know someone who can help, point them my way. Help me get the word out.

items.title: Sridhar ji highlights three very important areas in the podcast related to hardware which is also applicable for PCB manufacturing- 1. Speciality chem...
items.description_html:
Sridhar ji highlights three very important areas in the podcast related to hardware which is also applicable for PCB manufacturing-

1. Speciality chemicals used in PCB.
We have been working closely with few R&D startups in this space- copper nanomaterials(Anuna Labs) and others. Its very important that we build this in India. Huge gap/opportunity.

2. CAD used in PCB- Spell from @PcbCupid is one step in this direction with a larger vision of building our own CAD tools for PCB. Team has made good progress on autorouting recently.

3. Test and Measurement Tools - Huge gap in this space and almostly entirely imported products today. TDR used in impedance measurment for example is a low hanging fruit in PCB space if someone wants to start.

All of these are important for building a strong electronics ecosystem in 🇮🇳


Sridhar Vembu: One company I hugely admire is NPCI, and every time I use UPI, I think of their work and the massive impact! So it is an honour to do the podcast with their CEO Shri Dilip Asbe. Thank you Dilip-ji🙏

items.title: Exactly 10 years ago in May 2016, i went to a session on bootstrapping at TiE event in Santa Clara. That changed my life. When i spent time in bay are...
items.description_html:
Exactly 10 years ago in May 2016, i went to a session on bootstrapping at TiE event in Santa Clara. That changed my life.

When i spent time in bay area working on advanced sensing technologies in semicon space, everything was there in life(money, granted patents, travelling to new country every month, wife, so called good life) but it still felt empty within.

Was burning a lot of midnight oil trying to build something, failed twice trying to build in US. Those failures hit hard and made me realise that everthing that i had was only beacuse of the company that i was working for, i was zero outside of it.

During this phase is when i first met @svembu ji, did not know who he was, did not know about zoho. He was wearing a plain simple green kurtha, jeans and slippers gving a session on bootstraping his company. I asked "can we bootstrap a hardware business too ?" And he said " why not ? "

This along with zoho story, made my conviction of moving back to India stronger, it took almost a week for me to digest what zoho was and what was built without raising a single dime from VCs and i felt so proud and blessed to have met the man behind.

Moved back to India same year in October. It was only about building something here which makes sense in long term, something which was ignored by people for a long time.

Ofcourse it came at a cost, divorce, hard time telling people not to worry about me or my personal life, making sure that my parents are not suffering, isolating all the peronal stuff with the vision of building strong electronics manufacutring ecosystem in India.

10 years FF- we are a strong team of 200+, bootstrapped, growing fast, building factories in India which can compete globally soon. On a personal level, i still hardly spend few thousand rupess everymonth, lead a minimal life, have a loving wife who belives in me and a kid who is growing fast, parents who are proud that i am doing something in India.

There is no multimillion dollars in my bank as safety, my kid and wife are Indians with Indian passport and my parents were teachers/avg govt employee without any background in business.

I dont feel empty inside me, anymore. Fire inside me is for building India's electronics manufacturing capacity that can compete globally, a strong ecosystem for next gen to build products from India and a strong team that can run this beyond me.

Jai Hind!🇮🇳

items.title: RT Anil Agarwal: It is painful to see India face the adverse consequences of a war we have nothing to do with, particularly because of raw material se...
items.description_html:
RT Anil Agarwal
It is painful to see India face the adverse consequences of a war we have nothing to do with, particularly because of raw material security. We don’t have to import 90% of our oil, or 95% of our copper or 99.5% of our gold or any other metal resources. Mother Earth has given us the best geology. Last 40 years of practice in the minerals, metals and oil industry have taught me this. Now is the time, with this positive government, to give entrepreneurs freedom.

At just 19, I came to Bombay from Bihar and built a company in this industry from scratch. Vedanta was able to acquire Hindustan Zinc and BALCO under a privatisation programme which was never completed. 26% and 49% stakes are still held by the government and not been transferred as per a predetermined agreement. Vedanta also acquired ONGC Oil and Gas asset via UK’s Cairn and Sesa Goa Iron ore from Japan’s Mitsui.

In each one, the ambition and goal was to increase the production so much that India wouldn’t need to import but, in fact export. In Hindustan Zinc and BALCO, we succeeded. We increased zinc production 10 times, 20 times in aluminium. In the process, more than 1,000 companies have come up for processing this raw material. And Vedanta has contributed Rs 4.5 lakh crore to the exchequer in the last ten years. In oil and gas, our vision is to produce 1 million barrels per day. In iron ore, my vision is to produce 100 million tonnes, 33% of today’s production level. There are many more underperforming government assets that can perform the same way.

Three or four decades ago, there was no know-how, no experts, no finance in this industry. It took a lot of determination. Vedanta raised 35 billion USD from abroad and invested in India. It brought experts and technology. Because of that, Hindustan Zinc can also produce silver and fertiliser, and we are working very hard to start producing critical minerals very soon, something we were told would never happen in India.

Globally, only few very large companies engage in mining as it is very difficult without scale. Vedanta should be for India what Rio Tinto and BHP are for Australia or Vale is for Brazil. Other entrepreneurs and companies must create new Vedantas.

Most of the advanced countries have become prosperous by developing natural resources, including oil and gas. In India, the system has to stop creating hurdles. It needs to take a long-term view, as this government is not revenue-minded but, production-minded. It must facilitate. Instead of notices and judicial activity, all businesses need to receive recognition and respect. Benefit of doubt should pass onto them.

In India, we have economic heroes who can raise production equal to the biggest and best in the world. We should not allow tunnel vision or outdated mindset to keep us small and dependent. Some may remember the case of Rio Tinto which had invested in a diamond mine in Madhya Pradesh. It could have made India a global hub for diamond production, but they exited because of a complex, interfering and discouraging system.

The most important thing is trust. Keep the system simple, keep regulation limited and you eliminate the root cause of wrongdoing. We should have self-certification instead of lengthy approvals and clearances. The Government issues the rulebook and entrepreneurs strictly comply, subject to audit. Constant enquiry and interference only discourage enterprise. Also, the mindset of discrimination between public sector and private sector should change.

This year my family had the biggest tragedy. It was heartening to hear the words of the Prime Minister who said, “You must stay strong and continue to do work which is important for India.” That is the motivation of my life. I have the blessings of my grandfather and grandmother, who spent 40 years in Vrindavan in a small hut, in Manav Seva Sangh with their guruji, Swami Sharnanand Ji Maharaj. They always advised me, if you have wealth, you must use it for society. If you don’t have wealth, you must always help society with your hand. We all are willing to work hard, take risks and ensure that India becomes self-sufficient. The system should encourage us to deliver for our society and nation; this will eradicate unemployment and encourage women empowerment. This sector has given the maximum revenue to the exchequer in India and in the world for nation-building. It will help in making our country a developed nation, as envisioned by our Prime Minister. Let’s make India & Indians proud.

items.title: Our entire country's 🇮🇳 production of bare pcb is equivalent of jlc capacity. If you take only multilayer, than its worse than this. Jlc is not ...
items.description_html:
Our entire country's 🇮🇳 production of bare pcb is equivalent of jlc capacity.
If you take only multilayer, than its worse than this.

Jlc is not even in the top 50 in 🇨🇳.

Let that sink in...

China was closed for 15days last month, we were overbooked by 3-4x and we couldn't get the machines for the bottleneck process on time.

Raw materials, capital goods everything has to come from 🇨🇳 and it takes time.

Factory in china can get these machines in 7 days to expand, we need to wait atleast 45-60 days.

What you see as an outcome is delays! Because most of the people serach for 🇮🇳 fab during Chinese New Year and Chinese equivalent of dushera season. Most of the fabs in India run overcapacity during this time.

We have grown by more than 3x this year and we remain focused on solving problems that we have,(delay being the major one)

We have also made great progress on our new 10x larger new plant and hope we handle the next season better.

Thanks for your support and patience. Always grateful 🙏

items.title: RT Hemant Mohapatra: Ok, so for anyone who's upset about the @SarvamAI models not beating GPT5/other 1T parameter models, my response: are you f kiddi...
items.description_html:
RT Hemant Mohapatra
Ok, so for anyone who's upset about the @SarvamAI models not beating GPT5/other 1T parameter models, my response: are you f kidding me? A year ago you were crying about India not having our own LLMs trained from scratch. The team has launched 14 amazing products in 14 days and it took years of work, each. And they span text, OCR, voice, LLMs, STT, hardware! I mean hardware! Edge models! Celebrate. They are just getting started. Sure, I am biased as their first investor, but we invested because we thought so highly of them. Of course we are biased. And the odds were enormous. But the opportunity is enormous-er :-) and these guys are absolute killers, and the team hunts! I haven't chatted with @pratykumar today but I heard someone say he saw that PPT for today's LLM launch session literally ON THE STAGE for the first time and laid it all out without a prompter. You can only do it when you know your material cold, and your heart is 100% living and breathing this stuff. Just incredible, congrats to everyone at @SarvamAI. Start of an era.

items.title: India's urban real estate problems are slowly being moved to rural as well, by the same reasons mentioned in this post. In 2021, one acre in Dabbaspet...
items.description_html:
India's urban real estate problems are slowly being moved to rural as well, by the same reasons mentioned in this post.

In 2021, one acre in Dabbaspet industrial area was 1.5cr /KIADB. This was alloted at 37.5L (75% discount) for specific section of people without any limit on it.

Today, one acre in the same KIABD area is 15cr!. Less than 5 years and there are still soo many vacant lands, not much industries running. But the prices are so high, that it's almost impossible for factories to be setup there.

Such artificial price hike up, mostly driven by government is not good for industrial growth!

We need to learn from China.

items.title: P/B ratio has historically been a more reliable and stable valuation metric for predicting future returns compared to alternatives such as P/E or EV/E...
items.description_html:
P/B ratio has historically been a more reliable and stable valuation metric for predicting future returns compared to alternatives such as P/E or EV/EBITDA. I had demonstrated this through correlation analysis in an earlier investor memo.

The chart below tracks the P/B ratio (blue line) of the Nifty Smallcap 100 Index against the 25th percentile of its trailing 5-year P/B range (orange line). In simple terms, when the blue line falls below the orange line, the current valuation is in the lowest quartile of the past five years.

Since P/B is broadly proportional to ROE, the structural improvement in index ROE—from ~8–12% in the previous decade to ~15% currently—has led to a gradual upward shift in the orange line over time.

Notably, for the first time since the COVID crash, the current P/B has moved below this 25th percentile threshold. Historically, similar instances—during the 2011–2013 downturn, Q1 2016 correction, and the COVID crash—have been followed by strong returns over the subsequent 1–3 years.

This does not imply that markets have bottomed, nor does it guarantee short-term gains. In an event-driven environment, especially with geopolitical risks such as an escalation in war, markets could decline further by 10–20%.

However, what can be said with a reasonably high degree of confidence is that forward-looking returns over a 1–3 year horizon from such valuation levels have historically been better than average.

items.title: In my experience, this is probably the first time the Mid & Small cap indices have outperformed the Nifty 50 during a crisis time. It's also extremely...
items.description_html: In my experience, this is probably the first time the Mid & Small cap indices have outperformed the Nifty 50 during a crisis time. It's also extremely rare to see the Mid & Small cap indices (avg of the two) beating the Nifty 50 near the bottom of the cycle. Their avg CAGR has outperformed during the last two tough years as well as all the below trailing years. I am not that surprised as their balance sheet, P&L and cash flow quality has substantially improved post COVID. I believe the biggest multi-baggers will emerge from this space as the earnings growth differential will be significant going forward. Because of the quality differential today vs during pre-COVID period, I would be surprised if their valuations correct to levels seen during the previous crashes. We probably have the best corporate quality and least stressed financials currently compared to any other previous big crisis.

items.description_html: Earnings growth picture for Dec'25 quarter vs Dec'24 quarter. Wtd Avg (growth in sum total earnings) is significantly better for the mid and small cap universe. Small cap has an exceptional earnings from Kiri Ind with 5000 cr plus (vs 177 cr year ago) which skews the growth much higher than normal. If you remove that, the wtd avg growth is 14.9%. Median growth reduces influence of such outliers or exceptional items and hence gives a better directional picture. Over all growth is good but nothing exceptional. I believe we should see much improved growth in the Mar'26 quarter as sharp pick up in economic activity (as covered in my last memo) started in the month of December 2025.

items.title: As seen in the table from my latest memo, drawdowns in small caps during the January–March period—measured from the January peak to the February/Mar...
items.description_html:
As seen in the table from my latest memo, drawdowns in small caps during the January–March period—measured from the January peak to the February/March trough—are not an exception; they are a recurring feature of market cycles. Just as consistently, these phases have been followed by sharp recoveries once the market establishes a bottom in February or March.
Current sentiment is such that investors are overly focused on the negatives, making the environment appear so foggy that positives have become invisible. This fog, as always, will eventually lift.

While market bottoms and the depth of drawdowns can never be predicted, it is highly probable that market levels a few months from now will be disproportionately higher. Unfortunately, short-term declines are unsettling for investors, causing many to step aside and miss the eventual strong upswing.

In hindsight, this phase will look like an obvious opportunity. But while living through it, investors see only risks and landmines—never the runway for the next flight

items.title: Markets move through extremes on both sides. These extremes are largely driven by sentiment and, at times, reinforced by fundamentals. Currently, Indi...
items.description_html:
Markets move through extremes on both sides. These extremes are largely driven by sentiment and, at times, reinforced by fundamentals. Currently, Indian equities are experiencing extremely weak sentiment, driven primarily by disappointment over the US trade deal and subdued earnings growth over the past few quarters. However, the September 2025 quarter has shown encouraging signs of earnings growth.

The on-ground feedback I am receiving across various domestic businesses is very strong. For many companies, activity has picked up significantly this month. In my career, I have rarely seen such a wide divergence between market sentiment and on-ground business momentum. After a long time, deep value is emerging in several pockets, even though valuations at the overall market level are not cheap. A shift in sentiment will require a trigger. If not a trade deal, strong earnings and supportive management commentary could serve as a meaningful catalyst.

Wishing everyone a very Happy New Year and hoping for a far more rewarding 2026!

items.title: There's a reason why D2C brands are growing so rapidly in India. Walk into many urban households today, and you'll notice the shift: • Floor cleaners...
items.description_html:
There's a reason why D2C brands are growing so rapidly in India.

Walk into many urban households today, and you'll notice the shift:

• Floor cleaners & home care: Legacy brands → Koparo
• Shampoo & personal care: Legacy brands → Plum
• Protein: Avvatar, PLIX, The Whole Truth
• Nutrition: Protein bars from Stroom, The Whole Truth & others
• Snacks: Protein & baked chips replacing traditional chips
• Instant meals: Health-focused noodles replacing conventional instant noodles

I feel these brands have two big advantages:

Thoughtful positioning - Non-toxic, plant-friendly home care (in Protein/fibre-rich foods - No palm oil, cleaner labels and better ingredients)

Quick commerce - Distribution is no longer a bottleneck. With 10–20 minute delivery, these brands now have mass reach and are just a tap away.

Indian consumers are increasingly paying for health, clean ingredients, and convenience—not just legacy brand names.

India's FMCG landscape is quietly undergoing a generational shift.

items.title: India has learned one lesson repeatedly: strategic dependence comes at a cost. - 90% of our oil demand is met through imports → We must diversify ene...
items.description_html:
India has learned one lesson repeatedly: strategic dependence comes at a cost.

- 90% of our oil demand is met through imports → We must diversify energy sources.
- AI models are largely built overseas and are weaponized or restricted (US restricted Mythos new models to outside countries or companies) → We need sovereign Indian AI models - India rolls out three sovereign AI models, Sarvam AI, Gnani AI, BharatGen, to take on Big Tech
* Semiconductors and rare earths have been external dependencies → India is now investing in domestic chip manufacturing and strengthening its rare earth ecosystem (China had weaponized rare earth exports)

Energy security. Technology security. Supply chain security.

The next decade won't just be about economic growth—it will be about strategic self-reliance!

items.title: At Data For India, we spend a lot of time on big questions about India: female employment, household spending, fertility, health, the economy, and mor...
items.description_html:
At Data For India, we spend a lot of time on big questions about India: female employment, household spending, fertility, health, the economy, and more. These often require deep research, large datasets, and long-form insights.

But sometimes, you just want a clear answer to one sharp question:
When will India’s population stop growing?
What makes up India’s GDP?
Which diseases affect Indians the most today?

We are now ready to answer those questions for you.

Introducing Data Bytes: one chart about India every week, and the story behind the data.

Every Monday, starting 6th July, we will answer one simple question you might have about India, through one easy-to-understand chart and the context you need to interpret and use the information.

You can already read the first three Data Bytes on our platform, and subscribe now to get the next one in your inbox: https://www.dataforindia.com/data-bytes/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

If there is a question about India you would like us to answer, write to us at info@dataforindia.com.

items.title: Rainfall shapes nearly every dimension of India's economy, ecology and food systems. Rain-fed agriculture occupies over half of India's cultivated lan...
items.description_html:
Rainfall shapes nearly every dimension of India's economy, ecology and food systems. Rain-fed agriculture occupies over half of India's cultivated land, making the timing and distribution of rainfall critical for agricultural production, rural livelihoods and national food security.

Unlike India's temperature trends, where we see a clear increase in average temperatures over time, the total annual rainfall across India has remained broadly stable since the early twentieth century.

On average, India receives between 1,000 and 1,300 mm of rainfall over the course of a year based on the all-India long-term average calculated over the period of 1901-2024. Over the past century, there have been considerable year-to-year fluctuations, but the cumulative annual rainfall has remained broadly stable, showing only a weak and statistically insignificant decline.

Rainfall data in India comes from observational records maintained by the India Meteorological Department (IMD). These datasets combine observations from thousands of rain gauges across India and extend back to 1901.

Despite this stability, rainfall is becoming more concentrated and erratic in several regions.

How often has India’s annual rainfall diverged from long-term “normal” levels? Read Juhi Chatterjee’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/long-term-rainfall/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Rainfall #Rain #Climate #India #DataForIndia

items.title: What Chamath is saying below is not wrong, but it is nothing new - maybe 2 years late, and doesn't add to the discourse. Yes, the old model of T&M (bi...
items.description_html:
What Chamath is saying below is not wrong, but it is nothing new - maybe 2 years late, and doesn't add to the discourse. Yes, the old model of T&M (billing by the hour) is ending and shifting towards outcomes based pricing. The cost of new code generation is down by 80%, but it comes at its own cost (resources and community cost).

If the Enterprise world were to be built completely from scratch today, the "AI disrupts everything" argument would be true. As things stand today, there is a massive delta between how exponentially fast AI models are improving and how fast legacy multi-billion-dollar enterprises can meaningfully absorb them. This is creating a massive Enterprise deployment gap (refer image below from Infosys AI Investor day). This gap is a $300-400 Billion opportunity for Technology Services companies - albeit not IT players would benefit from this. There should be good value for IT players who can deliver the conversion layer with an AI-ready approach.

So far, the ROI for companies using AI instead of humans is not there and this may change in another year if the AI capex does not slow down. But as investors, at some point, we must ask - is the pessimism on IT running ahead of Enterprise reality?


Chamath Palihapitiya: Show me the incentive and I’ll show you the outcome.

"In the wake of Gulf crisis, the import-linked cost of a 14.2 kg cylinder rose above ₹1,600, yet the household price was held near ₹900, and lower still for the poorest."

Amidst the headlines of last 2 years - tariffs, AI, wars - one may miss the significance of a (rare) synchronous monetary and fiscal policy in action with a goal to support domestic consumption. Those who extol the China model will never understand the pay-off that comes from protecting bottom of pyramid consumption.

items.title: Thought provoking talk on portfolio risk management. Mark Spitznagel of Universa Investments explains that dedicating 3% of a stock portfolio to true ...
items.description_html:
Thought provoking talk on portfolio risk management. Mark Spitznagel of Universa Investments explains that dedicating 3% of a stock portfolio to true "crash insurance" (deep OTM puts) boosts long-term compound growth better than relying on traditional "safe havens" like gold, Treasuries, or currencies, which often act as "imposters" by failing to deliver during actual crashes.

March 2008 - March 2020 period: A portfolio of 96.7% S&P 500 + 3.3% Universa tail-risk insurance delivered 11.5% annualized CAGR, versus 7.9% for 100% S&P 500 - a +3.6 percentage point improvement.

The 3.3% sleeve kept the overall portfolio essentially unscathed in March 2020 (when the S&P 500 fell 12.4%), while Universa itself returned +3,612% that month (+4,144% YTD).

Note: This is not a general recommendation.


0xDipper: Mark Spitznagel - founder of Universa, the "black swan" fund Nassim Taleb advises - on the most counterintuitive idea in investing:

add a 3% sliver of crash insurance to a stock portfolio. give that insurance a 0% expected return - it makes nothing over time.

it still raises

items.description_html:
Agree with the problem being pointed out, but not with Kunal's end remark that there is no word (read as no concept) of productivity or efficiency in Indian languages.

Here is one:
उत्पादकत्व (Utpādakatva) - Noun
The Sanskrit word for "productivity", which is derived from the root utpādana (meaning to produce, generate, or create).

The problem is that Indians have lost the zeal for greatness which used to define us once. There are pockets of change in youngsters, but the problem is quite real en-masse.

items.title: We can think of the (build vs borrow) debate on India AI from the lens of Theory of Reflexivity. In a reflexive system, the choice alters the future r...
items.description_html:
We can think of the (build vs borrow) debate on India AI from the lens of Theory of Reflexivity. In a reflexive system, the choice alters the future reality. If we dont try, we feed more data to US LLMs, denting our chances the more we delay. Now that we have some funding coming in (say Sarvam), the next leg of human talent and chips etc would also happen gradually.

But choosing not to do it - kills that possibility completely. So I also agree - we need both.


Roshan Cariappa 🇮🇳: Should India adopt AI or build its own?

I think we're asking the wrong question.

Because, the real answer is we need both.

A few thoughts on why Adoption and Sovereignty are complements, not substitutes.

WDYT?

Bengal's jute sector is witnessing a broader revival. Three more closed jute mills—Baranagar Jute Mill, Jagaddal Jute Industries and EMCO Jute Mill—are set to reopen, restoring jobs for over 7,000 workers. This follows the reopening of Naihati and Kankinara jute mills, which together are expected to bring back around 5,500 jobs, taking the total employment restoration to more than 12,500 workers across five mills.

Government of West Bengal has set a July 10 target to reopen all currently closed jute mills. If achieved, it would mark one of the largest coordinated revivals of Bengal's traditional jute industry in recent years, bringing thousands of workers back to production and signalling renewed momentum for a sector that remains central to the state's industrial economy.

items.title: RT Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port, Kolkata: Transforming Kolkata’s Riverfront: Blending Modern Infrastructure with Heritage On 27.06.2026, Syama Prasad ...
items.description_html:
RT Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port, Kolkata
Transforming Kolkata’s Riverfront: Blending Modern Infrastructure with Heritage

On 27.06.2026, Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port, Kolkata (SMPK) had the privilege of hosting an interactive session with Shri Sanjeev Sanyal
Member, Economic Advisory Council to the Prime Minister (EAC-PM).

The discussion centered on a transformative vision for the Hooghly Riverfront—stretching from Maa er Ghat (Bagbazar) to Millennium Park Ghat. The focus is to create a vibrant, people-centric public space while ensuring sustainable urban development and seamless city connectivity.

💡 A Vision of Living Heritage:
During the session, Shri Sanyal strongly advocated for upgrading the quality of urban life while strictly preserving the old essence of both sides of the Hooghly. He emphasized that iconic elements of Kolkata’s cultural fabric—such as the historic Mullick Ghat Flower Market and the traditional Kushti Akhras (wrestling arenas)—should be modernized and integrated, rather than replaced.
The high-level session was attended by:

Shri Rathendra Raman, Chairperson, SMPK

Shri Samrat Rahi, Deputy Chairperson, SMPK

Shri Devidatta Satpathy, Chief Vigilance Officer, SMPK

Senior Port Officials, distinguished urban experts, and representatives from The Bengal Chamber of Commerce and Industry (BCC&I).

Together, SMPK and its partners are committed to shaping a greener, more accessible, and culturally rooted waterfront for the City of Joy.

@shipmin_india
@PIB_ShipMin
@PIB_India
#SMPK #RiverfrontDevelopment #HooghlyRiver #UrbanTransformation #SustainableDevelopment #MaritimeIndia #KolkataHeritage #EACPM

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today we are going to discuss a very unique 2W OEM that has been able to built a product that is built to last in 10+ years. For con...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today we are going to discuss a very unique 2W OEM that has been able to built a product that is built to last in 10+ years. For context - They had no legacy nothing and have built the business ground up from nothing (product first distribution later)

Ather invested in the things that compound and are hard to copy i.e. design IP, software, brand, charging network and rented the things that don't (cell manufacturing). That is why it spends less than Ola yet has better unit economics, and why the moat is earned rather than bought.

And this is the fruits of investing in building technology and a superior product for years before just thinking about scaling distribution. At the end of the day we can see the result that the product won that did not catch fire :)

But here today we will get into details of how they created this MOAT and how it is now able to win a superior market share and show such a strong growth at the same time when competition did not decrease in fact it has gone up!

items.description_html:
RT Saurabh Chandra
It was a great show at Automate for @atirobotics. There is a clear vibe shift in robotics demand in US manufacturing. There is a huge intent to get robots now compared to earlier when people were evaluating robots.

While our pallet mover and Tugger were our workhorses with folks looking to deploy immediately, the show stealer was our industrial humanoid, Sherpa Mecha. Many people were surprised to see we did the actuators and had a very novel arm design (motors in shoulder, cable driven, carbon fiber arms) compared to most folks who have put together Chinese arms with software on top. All our products are made with first principles thinking and this is no different. Focused usecases and real deployments will win the market.

We will get to real deployments much faster (next summer) and beta deployments start this year itself. We already have a global customer in both US and India where we will be deploying the Mecha.

We also move into our new facility to assemble the robots near Detroit in July.

Never been a better time to be in robotics. Super excited!

items.title: RT Megha Patnaik: new blog post at @Ideas4India 45% of India’s manufacturing is informal. My work shows GST is slowly changing this: an input credit ...
items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
new blog post at @Ideas4India 45% of India’s manufacturing is informal. My work shows GST is slowly changing this: an input credit system creates a formalisation cascade where firms demand invoices from suppliers & this pulls the entire supply chain slowly into the formal economy


Ideas for India: Value-added tax has a self-enforcing design that generates formalisation cascades through supply chains. Using @_CMIE Prowess data on 12,024 firms for the period 2013-2022, Megha Patnaik @megonomista examines impact of India’s introduction of GST in 2017
https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/macroeconomics/how-the-gst-created-a-formalisation-cascade

items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
Global Capability Centres are projected to contribute to 3.5% of India's GDP. My new paper argues that GCCs are firm-level reorganisation at the global scale: only the most productive firms set up GCCs, trading off lower wages against higher coordination costs of remote work.


CEPR: New CEPR Discussion Paper - DP21294
Services Across Borders: How Firms Organize Remote Work at Scale
Megha Patnaik @megonomista @UniLUISS
https://ow.ly/2gIf50YuL4m
#CEPR_ITRE #EconTwitter

items.title: RT Megha Patnaik: 45% of India's GDP is informal. My new paper shows GST's credit mechanism is pulling firms into the formal economy, not through enfo...
items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
45% of India's GDP is informal. My new paper shows GST's credit mechanism is pulling firms into the formal economy, not through enforcement but because every buyer demands an invoice from the supplier behind it.
Formalisation cascading begins with large firms and grows upstream


CEPR: New CEPR Discussion Paper - DP21163
Self-Enforcing Tax Design and Supply Chain Formalization: Evidence from India’s GST Reform
Megha Patnaik @megonomista @UniLUISS
https://ow.ly/5QK550YfcL1
#CEPR_PE #EconTwitter

items.title: Nuvama trims estimates on Jindal Steel: 👉 FY27/FY28 EBITDA cut by 9.5%/9.6% on lower volumes & higher cost of production/tn 👉 Maintains REDUCE w...
items.description_html:
Nuvama trims estimates on Jindal Steel:
👉 FY27/FY28 EBITDA cut by 9.5%/9.6% on lower volumes & higher cost of production/tn
👉 Maintains REDUCE with a target price of ₹1,018 vs ₹1,154

Key reasons:
1⃣ Long products (~48% of volumes) have seen a sharp price correction, while HRC prices are yet to adjust, pressuring Q2FY27 earnings.
2⃣ Higher costs are expected to weigh on Q1FY27.
FY27 volume estimates lowered due to weak H1 demand and a slower ramp-up of the 4.6 mtpa blast furnace.
3⃣ Meaningful cost savings from captive coal & pellet usage have yet to materialize.

CareEdge’s latest sector update points to a clear shift in industry dynamics, where growth is becoming more concentrated, and more competitive.

Key takeaways:

-Retail-led momentum strengthening: Industry premiums grew 8.7% y-o-y in May 2026, driven by health (↑13.7%) and motor segments, with retail health alone expanding 30.5% y-o-y.

-Private insurers & SAHIs gaining share: Private players grew 11.8%, while SAHIs surged 31.7%, pushing their combined market share to 70.9%—highlighting a decisive tilt toward customer-centric, retail-focused models.

-Segment divergence persists: Sharp contractions in fire (-24.5%) and crop insurance continue to offset broader gains, underscoring volatility in commercial and government-linked lines.

Read Report: https://www.careratings.com/uploads/newsfiles/1782196581_Health%20and%20Private%20Insurers%20Lead%20Non-Life%20Premium%20Growth%20in%20May%202026.pdf

After nearly three years of pressure, the Indian cotton yarn industry is seeing a meaningful reset, driven as much by supply correction as demand recovery.

Key takeaways:

- Structural capacity rationalisation: ~20% of spinning capacity (10–12 mn spindles) has exited, tightening supply and improving pricing power for organised players.

- Demand revival with global linkages: Stronger export flows, especially China resurgence, easing U.S. tariff-related uncertainties and improved sentiment around trade agreements are supporting order books.

- Margin recovery underway: Yarn spreads have rebounded to ~₹120–125/kg, with operating margins expected to expand by 150–200 bps in FY27.

Read Report Now: https://lnkd.in/gdNV2wmh

#CottonYarn #TextileSector #IndiaManufacturing #ExportTrends #SupplyChains #MarginExpansion #CommodityMarkets #ChinaDemand #TradeAgreements #CareEdgeRatings

items.title: 🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE. Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may...
items.description_html:
🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE.

Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may be inflating exports a bit (from inventories). But whatever that contribution is, clearly June oil exports have surged to >95% of pre-war levels (and some tanker trackers have UAE June oil exports even above pre-war levels).

With exports surging, the recovery in oil wellhead production won't be far behind, me thinks.

Importantly, UAE oil is flowing well beyond its typical trade partners. With Chinese demand down, Abu Dhabi is shipping lots of crude into Europe and the US.

items.title: US total oil production -- and crude output -- rose to a record high in April, according to revised @EIAgov data. The monthly data pegs US total oil o...
items.description_html:
US total oil production -- and crude output -- rose to a record high in April, according to revised @EIAgov data.

The monthly data pegs US total oil output at 21.836m b/d in April (up 565,000 b/d from the initial estimate for the month based on weekly data). On an annual basis, US total oil production is up >1m b/d.

April crude production was revised higher to 13.934m b/d (up 348,000 b/d from the weekly data) while NGLs output was also revised up, to 7.903m b/d (up 218,000 b/d from weekly data).

items.title: Retail investors are stepping away from Magnificent 7 stocks: Retail investors accounted for just 6% of total Magnificent 7 trading volume over the 5 ...
items.description_html:
Retail investors are stepping away from Magnificent 7 stocks:

Retail investors accounted for just 6% of total Magnificent 7 trading volume over the 5 trading days ending Friday, the lowest in 4 years.

Individual investors showed less interest in Magnificent 7 stocks over the last week than on roughly 85% of trading days since 2022.

By comparison, retail trading accounted for more than 20% of Magnificent 7 volume during some periods in 2023 and 2024, and remained above 15% for most of 2025.

As a result, the 5-day moving average of retail trading volume in Magnificent 7 stocks is down to ~25 million shares, the lowest in at least 18 months.

This marks a -79% decline from the April 2025 peak of ~120 million shares.

Retail interest is shifting from the Magnificent 7 toward semiconductor stocks.

items.title: BREAKING: Total crypto card deposits have officially crossed above $10 billion for the first time in history. This puts total deposits up +82% year-to...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Total crypto card deposits have officially crossed above $10 billion for the first time in history.

This puts total deposits up +82% year-to-date and nearly +250% year-over-year.

The surge comes amid a rapid adoption of stablecoins as a payment rail, particularly in cross-border transactions.

Yesterday, Visa, Mastercard, and a consortium of over 140 companies jointly launched a new USD-pegged stablecoin called Open USD, which is set to accelerate this trend.

Onchain platform Jupiter Mobile has also driven adoption, which has seen +65% month-over-month growth in new card users.

User growth has been fueled by localized integrations such as QR-based payments and growing accessibility across 60+ countries.

Crypto card adoption is rapidly expanding.

items.title: US job openings are rebounding: US job openings jumped +9,000 in May, to 7.6 million, the highest since May 2024. This follows a +698,000 surge in Apr...
items.description_html:
US job openings are rebounding:

US job openings jumped +9,000 in May, to 7.6 million, the highest since May 2024.

This follows a +698,000 surge in April, the biggest monthly addition since September 2022.

At the same time, total private job openings rose +2,000, to 6.8 million, the highest since March 2024, following a +635,000 increase in April.

As a result, the ratio of job openings to unemployed workers is up to 1.0, the highest since January 2025.

There are now 287,000 more available vacancies than unemployed workers, the largest difference since January 2025.

Is AI creating new jobs?

YoY, total production surged +1.01 million barrels per day, or +4.9%.

Crude oil production alone increased by +216,000 barrels per day in April, to a record 13.93 million barrels per day.

Since the 2008 Financial Crisis, total US petroleum output has QUADRUPLED.

Over the same period, crude oil production has soared +250%.

US oil production is booming.

items.title: The long US Dollar trade is crowded: Speculative long positioning in the US Dollar surged to +$34.3 billion as of June 23rd, the highest in 18 months....
items.description_html:
The long US Dollar trade is crowded:

Speculative long positioning in the US Dollar surged to +$34.3 billion as of June 23rd, the highest in 18 months.

This covers hedge funds and asset managers, who take positions based on price trends and macro views rather than to offset existing currency risk.

Bullish positioning has more than TRIPLED in just 7 weeks.

This also marks the 15th consecutive week of net long positioning, the longest streak since the 21-week run between 2024 and 2025.

Meanwhile, bearish Yen positions held by leveraged funds rose to 115,033 contracts in the week ended June 23rd, near the highest since November 2017.

The long US Dollar trade could reverse soon.

items.title: Why are central banks buying so much gold? A record 90% of central banks cited gold's performance during times of crisis as a key factor in their deci...
items.description_html:
Why are central banks buying so much gold?

A record 90% of central banks cited gold's performance during times of crisis as a key factor in their decision to hold gold, according to the World Gold Council survey of 69 central banks.

This trend is more frequently seen in emerging market and developing economy central banks, with 92% citing crisis performance as the primary driver.

By comparison, this figure stands at 81% among advanced economy central banks.

Furthermore, gold's role as a long-term store of value and inflation hedge was cited by 84% of respondents, while 83% pointed to its effectiveness as a portfolio diversifier.

Meanwhile, 85% of emerging market central banks cited gold as a geopolitical hedge, compared to just 56% in advanced economies.

Gold remains at the core of central bank financial reserves.

items.title: BREAKING: US M2 money supply surged +$247.8 billion in May, to a record $23.1 trillion. This marks the largest monthly increase since May 2021. Year-t...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: US M2 money supply surged +$247.8 billion in May, to a record $23.1 trillion.

This marks the largest monthly increase since May 2021.

Year-to-date, M2 has soared +$698.6 billion, the largest January to May increase in 5 years.

Money supply now stands $1.3 trillion above the March 2022 peak.

Since 2000, money in circulation has grown at an average annual rate of +6.3%.

US money creation is accelerating.

items.title: BREAKING: Global gold-backed ETFs posted -38.3 tonnes in outflows last week, the largest weekly outflow since September 2022. This was led by North Am...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Global gold-backed ETFs posted -38.3 tonnes in outflows last week, the largest weekly outflow since September 2022.

This was led by North America, at -23.6 tonnes, followed by Asia, at -8.7 tonnes, and Europe, at -5.9 tonnes.

In Dollar terms, these funds posted -$4.7 billion in withdrawals, the largest weekly outflow on record.

The largest US gold-backed ETF, $GLD, alone saw -$2.0 billion in outflows, the 4th-largest this year.

So far in June, $GLD has recorded -$3.2 billion in withdrawals, on track for its 2nd-worst month since February 2021, after a record -$8.5 billion in March.

Gold market sentiment remains highly volatile.

items.title: US chip stocks are on a historic run: The semiconductor index, $SOX, has outperformed the S&P 500 by +85 percentage points year-to-date, on pace for t...
items.description_html:
US chip stocks are on a historic run:

The semiconductor index, $SOX, has outperformed the S&P 500 by +85 percentage points year-to-date, on pace for the best half-year outperformance in history.

This would exceed the previous record set during the Dot-Com Bubble in H1 2000 by +15 percentage points.

This comes as $SOX has rallied +94% so far in 2026, compared to a +9% increase in the S&P 500.

Micron, $MU, has been a key driver, rallying +301% over this period, making it the largest contributor to the S&P 500's gains this year.

SanDisk, $SNDK, has soared +764% in H1, making it the top-performing stock in the index YTD.

The US chip stock boom is rewriting the record books.

items.title: RT Alex Veremeyenko: i'm obsessed with what's happening in AI reforestation right now this Franco-Brazilian startup called MORFO took a patch of land ...
items.description_html:
RT Alex Veremeyenko
i'm obsessed with what's happening in AI reforestation right now

this Franco-Brazilian startup called MORFO took a patch of land in Brazil that was rock-hard and compacted from years of cattle farming. they replanted it using a single drone. months later the ground was covered in grass, bushes, and small trees. the land came back to life.

here's how the whole thing works.

1. drones scan the terrain with high-resolution cameras and sensors
2. AI analyzes the imagery alongside soil samples, moisture levels, slope, and surrounding vegetation
3. the system picks from a catalog of 300+ native species, deciding exactly which plants will thrive in which specific spot
4. the drone fires biodegradable seed pods packed with seeds, nutrients, and moisture at 180 capsules per minute
5. satellite and drone imagery monitors regrowth over time, with AI tracking vegetation cover and biodiversity
6. two people and one drone cover 50 hectares a day. a person planting by hand manages about one hectare.

and MORFO isn't alone. AirSeed in Australia drops 250,000 seed pods per day into bushfire-scarred koala habitat, replanting swamp mahogany that koalas depend on to survive. Flash Forest in Canada fires 50,000 pods daily into wildfire-destroyed boreal forest, planning the replanting alongside Cree Indigenous communities. re-green won Prince William's Earthshot Prize after planting 6 million seedlings across 30,000 hectares of Amazon and Atlantic Forest.

five companies across four continents built this same approach independently. nobody coordinated. the physics of the problem demanded it.

knowing which seeds belong in which soil used to require years of ecological fieldwork, manual planting crews, and budgets that made large-scale restoration nearly impossible. now two people with a drone and an AI model trained on local soil data can replant 50 hectares before lunch.

this is the AI work that'll still matter in 50 years.

items.title: One thing we always track is the promoter's stake! When promoters keep increasing their stake, it often signals confidence in the business. Paisalo Di...
items.description_html:
One thing we always track is the promoter's stake!

When promoters keep increasing their stake, it often signals confidence in the business.

Paisalo Digital is a good example. Promoter holding has steadily increased from ~26% in FY19 to 46.72% in Q1FY27.

Also, the company has guided for doubling its AUM, income, and PAT over the next 3 years, and the stock is up 20% today.

Disclosure: We have Paisalo Digital in our FLUID Q Smallcase basket.

Overall growth however depicts strong resilience by the Indian economy amidst geopolitical uncertainties
